translation.content: Something exciting is happening on the margins of online club music. As one generation ages out, another, predominantly made up of Zoomers and Zillennials who fell into rave culture mid-pandemic or arrived via gaming, is on the rise. IRL and URL dance communities have long moved at different tempos, of course, but the generation gap today scans as fertile ground, particularly in America. Some of the most dialled-in electronic fans out there have threadbare links to formalised nightlife, their diet filled instead with DJ Ess, Jane Remover, Jet Set Radio iconography and a thriving ecosystem of global styles that might draw blank stares from anyone who settled into their preferences pre-2019. Leading the charge for what you might call post-club club enthusiasts—while pointedly reaffirming the value of connection as they go—are Dazegxd, gum.mp3 and Swami Sound, AKA EldiaNYC. The trio's blend of jungle, garage, R&B, vintage Black American dance music and screen-glued sub-genres hits like a shot of adrenaline. Swami Sound broke first, with sexy drill-laced 2-step edits that marked him as the New York outpost for a 2020s garage generation. In 2024, we named gum.mp3's Black Life, Red Planet as one of our favourite records, but it could just as easily have been Daze's exhibition mode; the pair's collaborative release, Girls Love Jungle, was a sleeper favourite in 2023, too. (Full disclosure: gum has contributed writing to Resident Advisor on Southern rap before.) Yet it's as a collective that the trio shine brightest, as we've explored in greater depth with our new feature this week. EldiaNYC are deeply committed to resource-sharing online, while stacking sets with startlingly fresh producers, some anchored to the parent eldia000 label and many still in their teens. They also give ample airtime to artists who you rarely hear in less adventurous hands. This might be the only RA Podcast to fold in soulful ghettotech from Mr De', head-in-the-clouds plugg from rising rapper 454, edits of both Bounty Killer and Toro Y Moi, and multiple acts (Guido YZ, synta3x, DJ B) who haven't registered on the series before. With one eye perhaps on the occasion, EldiaNYC nod to lineage, too. The trio kick off with a palette-cleansing Fred P double scoop, before ramping up steadily through slick forthcoming material and ironclad party anthems ("The Courts," "Acid Bit") on their way to a crescendo of footwork chops, baile drums and breakbeat delirium. Tip of a new wave in the US, it surely won't be long before Daze, gum and Swami have swept the international circuit. For now, RA.977 will leave your subs smoking. - Gabriel Szatan Hattie Lindert: What have you been up to lately? Daze: Working on a variety of projects and cool collaborations that I've been hoping to do for a while. Gum: I'm finishing up grad school and trying to work on a new solo album. Shopping a lot at the Polo store, too. Swami: I've been locked in daily finishing new music including this forthcoming four-track EP between myself and gum. At the same time working with my friends on branding and visual stuff to market the new music. It's been a steady process. How and where was this mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? Swami: The first hour is a back-to-back between gum and I. He showed up at my house at 9 AM—I was still in bed. The mix slightly resembles the direction we went for during our Chicago headline show in January, where we centred the flow around our new songs. Daze: I was out of town when they were recording their part but heard it and tried to match their flow while retaining my own personal charms. What's the key to your chemistry as a trio? The key to our chemistry is just keeping in touch with each other respectively. We check in at our own pace and come together when the time comes. What's one club or party that's had a major impact on you as an artist? Daze: The show that me and Gum performed at Catch One in Los Angeles on September 11th 2021 made me have faith in pursuing my musical career over staying in college. Swami: Saloon in Ebisu. I had an amazing time dancing the last time I was out there with the guys. Gum: My most recent show at Kieth Flack in Fukuoka. It feels amazing to have a supportive group of people on the other side of the world, and to have been able to play my favourite kind of music to a fun crowd. You're part of the international eldia collective, so who are some other eldia artists you're really impressed by? Swami: disctr4k, he's built for this stuff. Amazing mind. powerd0ll also made a remix to "Refuse" which definitely put me on a Riddim tip. Daze: Alongside Swami's picks of disctr4k & powerd0ll, I'm really enthralled by UNIT KAI & lyustra's productions. Gum: Ditto on disctr4k, he's the young GOAT. Highly skilled and deserving of support and praise. Most-prized record in your collection? Daze: Currently, Deadly Ammo EP featuring 16AJ, Kid B, Default and the late great General Malice. It was a gift from a dear friend and collaborator Anne Hero and as General Malice is one of my favourite junglists, so having one of his songs on wax means a lot to me. Swami: Person Pitch by Panda Bear—copped an OG press in Toronto. Gum: Black Mahogani II by Moodymann. It's one of my favourite albums ever by my favourite artist ever. I feel lucky to own so many Moodymann albums. What's one thing you can't live without when you're on tour? Gum: Tatcha Rice Wash and Eucerin Advanced Repair lotion. I have eczema, so I need both of those desperately. Swami: I gotta know where the best eats are. But on a more serious note: friends. That's why I love touring with Gum and Daze—it's never lonely. Daze: Friends, likewise. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? It's hard to pinpoint one cause: not only are all these struggles interconnected, they can be found the minute the average person picks up their phone. We encourage everyone to pick a cause, educate themselves and manifest their desire for a better world in their day-to-day actions. Looking ahead, what are you most excited for as a group this year? Whatever the future holds, there's nothing super concrete but the proliferation of our career as a group is more than enough.
translation.blurb: Raucous club jams from Dazegxd, gum.mp3 and Swami Sound, the trio setting pace for a new generation of electronic fans.
